OT4ML
Optimal Transport for Machine Learners
Chapters
Optimal Matching between Point CloudsMonge Problem between MeasuresKantorovich RelaxationDual ProblemSemi-discrete and Wasserstein-1Divergences and Dual NormsEntropic Regularization: Sinkhorn AlgorithmEntropic Regularization: ConvergenceGeneralized Wasserstein DistancesGeneralized OT ProblemsBeyond Comparing MeasuresDynamic Optimal TransportWasserstein Gradient FlowsGenerative Models via Transportation
ConclusionNotation TableIndex

Kantorovich Relaxation

Kantorovich’s relaxation is the decisive move that turns transport into convex optimization. Deterministic maps are replaced by couplings, infeasibility and asymmetry disappear, and the Wasserstein distances emerge. Historically, this linear-programming viewpoint grew from Kantorovich’s economic planning work Kantorovich, 1942 and is now the standard foundation of optimal transport Villani, 2003Villani, 2009Rachev & Rüschendorf, 1998.

from pathlib import Path
import sys

from IPython.display import Image as DisplayImage
from IPython.display import display

here = Path.cwd()
myst_dir = None
for candidate in [here, here.parent, here / "myst", here.parent / "myst", here.parent.parent / "myst"]:
    if (candidate / "ot4ml_web.py").exists():
        myst_dir = candidate.resolve()
        sys.path.insert(0, str(myst_dir))
        break

if myst_dir is None:
    raise RuntimeError("Could not locate myst/ot4ml_web.py")

repo_root = myst_dir.parent
thumbnails = repo_root / "notebooks-figures" / "thumbnails"

def show_book_figure(name, width=760):
    display(DisplayImage(filename=str(thumbnails / f"{name}.png"), width=width))

Discrete Relaxation¶

The discrete relaxation is the cleanest place to see mass splitting. It replaces permutations by a transportation polytope and reveals the linear-programming structure that algorithms exploit.

Monge’s discrete matching problem cannot be applied when the two clouds have different cardinalities or unequal weights. The continuous Monge problem has the same obstruction: there may be no map TTT such that T♯α=βT_\sharp\al=\beT♯​α=β, for instance when one Dirac mass must be sent to several Dirac masses. It is also asymmetric: two Dirac masses can be mapped to one, but one Dirac mass cannot be split into two by a deterministic map.

Kantorovich’s idea is to relax deterministic transportation. Instead of sending each source point xix_ixi​ to exactly one target, the mass at xix_ixi​ may be dispatched across several targets. The relaxation is encoded by a coupling matrix P∈R+n×mP\in\RR_+^{n\times m}P∈R+n×m​ for two discrete measures

α=∑iaiδxi,β=∑jbjδyj.\al=\sum_i a_i\delta_{x_i}, \qquad \be=\sum_j b_j\delta_{y_j}.α=i∑​ai​δxi​​,β=j∑​bj​δyj​​.
(1)
Definition: Discrete Couplings And Mass Conservation

Admissible couplings are constrained only by conservation of mass:

U(a,b):={P∈R+n×m  :  P1m=a,P⊤1n=b}.\CouplingsD(a,b) \eqdef \left\{ P\in\RR_+^{n\times m} \;:\; P\mathbf{1}_m=a,\quad P^\top\mathbf{1}_n=b \right\}.U(a,b):={P∈R+n×m​:P1m​=a,P⊤1n​=b}.
(2)

Equivalently, rows sum to the source masses and columns sum to the target masses:

(∑jPij)i=a,(∑iPij)j=b.\left(\sum_j P_{ij}\right)_i=a, \qquad \left(\sum_i P_{ij}\right)_j=b.(j∑​Pij​)i​=a,(i∑​Pij​)j​=b.
(3)

The first consequence is feasibility. There is always at least one admissible plan.

Definition: Discrete Product Coupling

Given weights a∈Δna\in\simplex_na∈Δn​ and b∈Δmb\in\simplex_mb∈Δm​, the discrete product, or trivial, coupling is

Pij⊗:=aibj.P^\otimes_{ij}\eqdef a_i b_j.Pij⊗​:=ai​bj​.
(4)

It belongs to U(a,b)\CouplingsD(a,b)U(a,b) and corresponds to choosing source and target labels independently.

The feasible set is a bounded intersection of an affine space with the nonnegative orthant, hence a convex polytope. In one dimension, the coupling can be read as a matrix: rows index source bins, columns index target bins, and the marginal constraints appear as prescribed row and column sums.

Proposition: Discrete Product Optimality Is Degenerate

Assume that all zero-mass rows and columns have been removed, so that ai>0a_i>0ai​>0 and bj>0b_j>0bj​>0, and let CCC be a finite cost matrix. The product plan P⊗=ab⊤P^\otimes=ab^\topP⊗=ab⊤ minimizes P↦⟨C,P⟩P\mapsto\langle C,P\rangleP↦⟨C,P⟩ over U(a,b)\CouplingsD(a,b)U(a,b) if and only if every coupling P∈U(a,b)P\in\CouplingsD(a,b)P∈U(a,b) minimizes it.

Proof

The reverse implication is immediate. Conversely, assume that P⊗P^\otimesP⊗ is optimal and let Q∈U(a,b)Q\in\CouplingsD(a,b)Q∈U(a,b) be arbitrary. Since all entries of P⊗P^\otimesP⊗ are positive, there exists t>0t>0t>0 small enough that

R:=(1+t)P⊗−tQR\eqdef(1+t)P^\otimes-tQR:=(1+t)P⊗−tQ
(5)

is nonnegative. It still has row sums aaa and column sums bbb, so R∈U(a,b)R\in\CouplingsD(a,b)R∈U(a,b). Also

P⊗=11+tR+t1+tQ.P^\otimes=\frac{1}{1+t}R+\frac{t}{1+t}Q.P⊗=1+t1​R+1+tt​Q.
(6)

Taking scalar products with CCC, the optimality of P⊗P^\otimesP⊗ forces both RRR and QQQ to have the same cost as P⊗P^\otimesP⊗. Since QQQ was arbitrary, all couplings are optimal.

Thus the product plan is mainly a feasibility witness. Except when the linear cost is constant on the whole transportation polytope, it is not expected to solve optimal transport.

show_book_figure("kantorovich-coupling-polylines")

Discrete couplings represented as straight transport segments. The deterministic graph is a feasible Monge-type plan, the product plan spreads every source mass over all targets, and the optimal Kantorovich plan minimizes the quadratic transport cost. Line width and opacity encode transported mass.

The interactive demo below separates the main feasible-plan archetypes: deterministic graphs, independent product couplings, sparse splitting plans, and entropic approximations.

Interactive panel. Use the point and mass sliders to see how a Kantorovich plan can split mass into several weighted links rather than choosing one destination per source.

show_book_figure("kantorovich-coupling-matrix-marginals")

Coupling matrices with their prescribed marginals. The central grayscale image displays PijP_{ij}Pij​; the red curve on the left is the source marginal aaa, and the blue curve on top is the target marginal bbb. The independent product plan is diffuse, whereas the one-dimensional optimal plan concentrates near the monotone quantile correspondence.

The companion control varies the bin count and the endpoint laws, making the transition from diffuse independence to monotone transport visually explicit.

Interactive panel. Use the problem-size and mass-shape controls to compare the coupling matrix with its red and blue marginal sums.

The Kantorovich feasible set is symmetric: P∈U(a,b)P\in\CouplingsD(a,b)P∈U(a,b) if and only if P⊤∈U(b,a)P^\top\in\CouplingsD(b,a)P⊤∈U(b,a). With a unit transport cost matrix CijC_{ij}Cij​, the discrete Kantorovich problem reads

LC(a,b):=min⁡P∈U(a,b)⟨C,P⟩=min⁡P∈U(a,b)∑i,jCijPij.\mathcal{L}_C(a,b) \eqdef \min_{P\in\CouplingsD(a,b)} \langle C,P\rangle = \min_{P\in\CouplingsD(a,b)} \sum_{i,j} C_{ij}P_{ij}.LC​(a,b):=P∈U(a,b)min​⟨C,P⟩=P∈U(a,b)min​i,j∑​Cij​Pij​.
(7)

This is a linear program, and its solutions need not be unique.

show_book_figure("kantorovich-permutation-versus-splitting")

From permutation matrices to splitting couplings. When the two empirical measures have the same number of atoms and uniform weights, an optimal plan can be a permutation matrix. Once target masses are nonuniform, one source can send mass to several targets and several sources can merge into the same target.

The interactive demo keeps the same source and target sites while changing the target mass imbalance, so the moment where permutation structure breaks becomes visible.

Interactive panel. Use the split-mass and geometry controls to contrast deterministic permutation-like transport with plans that divide source mass across targets.

Proposition: Sparse Optimal Plans

Assume ai>0a_i>0ai​>0, bj>0b_j>0bj​>0 and ∑iai=∑jbj=1\sum_i a_i=\sum_j b_j=1∑i​ai​=∑j​bj​=1. The linear program above admits an optimal coupling with at most n+m−1n+m-1n+m−1 nonzero entries.

Proof

The transportation polytope is compact, so a linear objective attains its minimum at an extreme point. Let PPP be an extreme point and let E={(i,j):Pij>0}E=\{(i,j):P_{ij}>0\}E={(i,j):Pij​>0} be its support graph on the bipartite vertex set of source and target indices. If this graph contains a cycle, put alternating signs +1,−1+1,-1+1,−1 on the cycle, obtaining a nonzero matrix HHH supported on EEE with zero row and column sums. For small t>0t>0t>0, both P+tHP+tHP+tH and P−tHP-tHP−tH are nonnegative couplings and PPP is their midpoint, contradicting extremality. Thus the support graph is a forest, which has at most n+m−1n+m-1n+m−1 edges.

Proposition: North-West Corner Feasible Plan

Let a∈R+na\in\RR_+^na∈R+n​ and b∈R+mb\in\RR_+^mb∈R+m​ have the same positive total mass. A greedy sweep constructs a coupling with at most n+m−1n+m-1n+m−1 positive entries: start at (i,j)=(1,1)(i,j)=(1,1)(i,j)=(1,1) with residual masses ri=air_i=a_iri​=ai​ and sj=bjs_j=b_jsj​=bj​, set

Pij=min⁡(ri,sj),P_{ij}=\min(r_i,s_j),Pij​=min(ri​,sj​),
(8)

subtract this value from both residuals, and advance every index whose residual has become zero. Repeat until all mass is exhausted.

Proof

All assignments are nonnegative. At each step, the mass placed in (i,j)(i,j)(i,j) is subtracted from one current row residual and one current column residual. An index is advanced only when its residual is fully filled. Each positive assignment exhausts at least one current row or column, so before the final assignment there can be at most n−1n-1n−1 row advances and m−1m-1m−1 column advances. The support is acyclic because once a row or column is advanced, it never appears again.

One-Dimensional Cases¶

In one dimension, the transportation polytope has a canonical monotone optimizer. This is the weighted version of the sorting rule from the matching chapter.

Proposition: One-Dimensional Weighted Sweep

Let x1≤⋯≤xnx_1\leq\cdots\leq x_nx1​≤⋯≤xn​ and y1≤⋯≤ymy_1\leq\cdots\leq y_my1​≤⋯≤ym​ be points on the line, and let c(x,y)=h(x−y)c(x,y)=h(x-y)c(x,y)=h(x−y) with hhh convex. The north-west corner plan between the sorted weighted atoms is optimal. Consequently, for unsorted one-dimensional inputs, an optimal plan is obtained in O(nlog⁡n+mlog⁡m)O(n\log n+m\log m)O(nlogn+mlogm) time by sorting and then sweeping the masses once from left to right.

Proof

The north-west plan is monotone: it cannot put positive mass on both (i,j)(i,j)(i,j) and (i′,j′)(i',j')(i′,j′) when i<i′i<i'i<i′ and j>j′j>j'j>j′. Conversely, any feasible plan with such a crossing pair can be improved by moving a small mass from (i,j)(i,j)(i,j) and (i′,j′)(i',j')(i′,j′) to (i,j′)(i,j')(i,j′) and (i′,j)(i',j)(i′,j). Convexity gives the required two-point inequality. Repeating this uncrossing procedure yields the unique monotone feasible plan, namely the sweep plan.

Permutation Matrices As Couplings¶

Now assume n=mn=mn=m and uniform weights a=b=1n/na=b=\mathbf{1}_n/na=b=1n​/n. For a permutation σ∈Perm(n)\sigma\in\Perm(n)σ∈Perm(n), let PσP_\sigmaPσ​ be its permutation matrix. The corresponding probability coupling is Pσ/nP_\sigma/nPσ​/n, and

⟨C,Pσ/n⟩=1n∑i=1nCi,σ(i).\langle C,P_\sigma/n\rangle = \frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n C_{i,\sigma(i)}.⟨C,Pσ​/n⟩=n1​i=1∑n​Ci,σ(i)​.
(9)

Thus the assignment problem is the minimization of a linear function over the discrete, non-convex set of permutation matrices. The convex relaxation is the Birkhoff polytope

Bn:={P∈R+n×n  :  P1n=1n,P⊤1n=1n}.\mathcal B_n \eqdef \left\{ P\in\RR_+^{n\times n} \;:\; P\mathbf{1}_n=\mathbf{1}_n,\quad P^\top\mathbf{1}_n=\mathbf{1}_n \right\}.Bn​:={P∈R+n×n​:P1n​=1n​,P⊤1n​=1n​}.
(10)
Definition: Extreme Points

For a compact convex set C\mathcal CC in a finite-dimensional vector space,

Extr⁡(C):={x∈C:x=(y+z)/2, y,z∈C⇒y=z=x}.\Extr(\mathcal C) \eqdef \{x\in\mathcal C: x=(y+z)/2,\ y,z\in\mathcal C \Rightarrow y=z=x\}.Extr(C):={x∈C:x=(y+z)/2, y,z∈C⇒y=z=x}.
(11)
Proposition: Linear Programs Have Extreme Minimizers

Let C\mathcal CC be nonempty and compact. For every linear form ℓ\ellℓ,

Extr⁡(C)∩arg min⁡x∈Cℓ(x)≠∅.\Extr(\mathcal C)\cap\argmin_{x\in\mathcal C}\ell(x)\neq\emptyset.Extr(C)∩x∈Cargmin​ℓ(x)=∅.
(12)
Proof

The minimizer set is nonempty, compact and convex. A nonempty compact convex set in finite dimension has an extreme point, obtained by taking a face of minimal affine dimension. That extreme point is also extreme in the original set.

Theorem: Birkhoff--von Neumann

The extreme points of Bn\mathcal B_nBn​ are exactly the permutation matrices.

Proof

A permutation matrix is extreme because its entries are in {0,1}\{0,1\}{0,1}. Conversely, let P∈BnP\in\mathcal B_nP∈Bn​ be non-integral. Consider the bipartite graph of entries 0<Pij<10<P_{ij}<10<Pij​<1. Every non-isolated vertex has degree at least two, so the graph contains a cycle. Put alternating signs along the cycle, giving a nonzero matrix HHH with zero row and column sums. For small ϵ>0\epsilon>0ϵ>0, both P+ϵHP+\epsilon HP+ϵH and P−ϵHP-\epsilon HP−ϵH remain bistochastic, and PPP is their midpoint. Thus PPP is not extreme.

Corollary: Kantorovich For Matching

If m=nm=nm=n and a=b=1n/na=b=\mathbf{1}_n/na=b=1n​/n, then the discrete Kantorovich problem admits an optimal solution of the form Pσ/nP_\sigma/nPσ​/n. The associated permutation solves the assignment problem.

For uniform empirical measures, the Kantorovich relaxation is tight: one can choose a permutation matrix among the minimizers. For general discrete measures, the Monge constraint may be empty and splitting couplings are essential.

Linear-Programming Algorithms¶

The discrete Kantorovich problem is a linear program with much more structure than a generic dense LP. Its variables are arcs of a complete bipartite network, its equality constraints are flow-conservation constraints, and its extreme points are sparse tree-like couplings.

Transportation Simplex And Network Simplex¶

The transportation simplex goes back to Dantzig’s formulation of the transportation problem Dantzig, 1951. It works on basic feasible couplings, whose support is completed into a spanning tree of the bipartite supply-demand graph. Reduced costs identify whether an unused arc can decrease the objective. Adding such an arc creates a unique cycle; one then pushes as much mass as possible around that cycle and removes the exhausted arc.

The network simplex is the corresponding pivoting method for general minimum-cost-flow problems Bertsekas & Eckstein, 1988. It keeps node potentials, reduced costs and a spanning-tree basis. Its worst-case number of pivots can be exponential, but the per-pivot operations exploit graph sparsity. Polynomial guarantees can be obtained from strongly polynomial minimum-cost-flow algorithms such as Orlin’s algorithm Orlin, 1997.

Interior-Point Methods¶

Generic interior-point methods approach the LP through a smooth central path. For the transport polytope, the logarithmic-barrier version is

Pϵ:=arg min⁡P1m=a,P⊤1n=bPij>0⟨C,P⟩−ϵ∑i,jlog⁡Pij.P_\epsilon \eqdef \argmin_{\substack{P\mathbf{1}_m=a,\;P^\top\mathbf{1}_n=b\\P_{ij}>0}} \langle C,P\rangle - \epsilon\sum_{i,j}\log P_{ij}.Pϵ​:=P1m​=a,P⊤1n​=bPij​>0​argmin​⟨C,P⟩−ϵi,j∑​logPij​.
(13)

The barrier is singular at the boundary, so each iterate stays strictly inside the transportation polytope. As ϵ↓0\epsilon\downarrow0ϵ↓0, the central path approaches the set of LP minimizers.

show_book_figure("kantorovich-log-barrier-lp-geometry")

Logarithmic-barrier central path for a triangular slice of a linear program. Large ϵ\epsilonϵ selects a central interior point; decreasing ϵ\epsilonϵ moves the minimizer toward the optimal vertex while never touching the boundary. This differs from entropic OT, where the entropy temperature is part of the regularized objective itself.

The interactive view exposes the barrier parameter directly: lowering ϵ\epsilonϵ slides the minimizer from the center of the feasible triangle toward the LP vertex.

Interactive panel. Use the barrier and angle controls to move along the interior central path of the transport polytope.

Both interior-point methods and Sinkhorn keep iterates positive, but they use positivity differently. Interior-point algorithms solve the original LP by decreasing a barrier parameter. Sinkhorn fixes an entropic temperature and solves a different, KL-regularized OT problem by alternating diagonal scalings.

Relaxation For Arbitrary Measures¶

This section lifts the finite-dimensional coupling matrix to a joint probability measure. The payoff is that existence, duality and metric properties can be stated for arbitrary laws, including discrete, singular and continuous distributions.

Continuous Couplings¶

Definition: Marginals Of A Joint Measure

Let π∈M+1(X×Y)\pi\in\Mm_+^1(\Xx\times\Yy)π∈M+1​(X×Y) and let PX(x,y)=xP_\Xx(x,y)=xPX​(x,y)=x and PY(x,y)=yP_\Yy(x,y)=yPY​(x,y)=y be the coordinate projections. The marginals of π\piπ are

π1:=(PX)♯π,π2:=(PY)♯π.\pi_1\eqdef(P_\Xx)_\sharp\pi, \qquad \pi_2\eqdef(P_\Yy)_\sharp\pi.π1​:=(PX​)♯​π,π2​:=(PY​)♯​π.
(14)

Equivalently, for bounded continuous test functions fff on X\XxX and ggg on Y\YyY,

∫f(x) dπ(x,y)=∫f dπ1,∫g(y) dπ(x,y)=∫g dπ2.\int f(x)\d\pi(x,y)=\int f\d\pi_1, \qquad \int g(y)\d\pi(x,y)=\int g\d\pi_2.∫f(x)dπ(x,y)=∫fdπ1​,∫g(y)dπ(x,y)=∫gdπ2​.
(15)
Definition: Couplings

Given α∈M+1(X)\al\in\Mm_+^1(\Xx)α∈M+1​(X) and β∈M+1(Y)\be\in\Mm_+^1(\Yy)β∈M+1​(Y), the set of couplings between α\alα and β\beβ is

Π(α,β):={π∈M+1(X×Y):π1=α, π2=β}.\Couplings(\al,\be) \eqdef \{\pi\in\Mm_+^1(\Xx\times\Yy):\pi_1=\al,\ \pi_2=\be\}.Π(α,β):={π∈M+1​(X×Y):π1​=α, π2​=β}.
(16)

This is the continuous analogue of the transportation polytope.

Remark: Probabilistic Interpretation Of Couplings

If X∼αX\sim\alX∼α and Y∼βY\sim\beY∼β, then π∈Π(α,β)\pi\in\Couplings(\al,\be)π∈Π(α,β) is the law of a pair (X,Y)(X,Y)(X,Y) whose coordinates have laws α\alα and β\beβ. The coupling encodes dependence. The tensor product α⊗β\al\otimes\beα⊗β corresponds to independence; a graph coupling (Id,T)♯α(\Id,T)_\sharp\al(Id,T)♯​α corresponds to the deterministic relation Y=T(X)Y=T(X)Y=T(X).

Unlike the Monge constraint, the coupling constraint is never empty. The continuous feasibility witness is the tensor product coupling.

Definition: Tensor Product And Trivial Coupling

Given α∈M+1(X)\al\in\Mm_+^1(\Xx)α∈M+1​(X) and β∈M+1(Y)\be\in\Mm_+^1(\Yy)β∈M+1​(Y), the tensor product coupling α⊗β\al\otimes\beα⊗β is defined by

∫h(x,y) d(α⊗β)(x,y)=∫X(∫Yh(x,y) dβ(y)) dα(x).\int h(x,y)\d(\al\otimes\be)(x,y) = \int_\Xx \left(\int_\Yy h(x,y)\d\be(y)\right) \d\al(x).∫h(x,y)d(α⊗β)(x,y)=∫X​(∫Y​h(x,y)dβ(y))dα(x).
(17)
Proposition: Product Optimality Is Degenerate

Assume that X\XxX and Y\YyY are compact metric spaces and that c∈C(X×Y)c\in\Cc(\Xx\times\Yy)c∈C(X×Y). The tensor product α⊗β\al\otimes\beα⊗β is optimal for inf⁡π∈Π(α,β)∫c dπ\inf_{\pi\in\Couplings(\al,\be)}\int c\d\piinfπ∈Π(α,β)​∫cdπ if and only if every coupling is optimal. This is also equivalent to the additive decomposition

c(x,y)=u(x)+v(y)c(x,y)=u(x)+v(y)c(x,y)=u(x)+v(y)
(18)

on the product support.

Proof Sketch

If all couplings are optimal, the product coupling is optimal. Conversely, assume the product is optimal. If cross differences failed to vanish on the product support, there would be points x0,x1,y0,y1x_0,x_1,y_0,y_1x0​,x1​,y0​,y1​ such that exchanging the two target neighborhoods decreases cost. Replacing a small amount of product mass on the two diagonal rectangles by mass on the crossed rectangles keeps the same marginals and lowers the cost, a contradiction. Vanishing cross differences imply c(x,y)=c(x,y⋆)+c(x⋆,y)−c(x⋆,y⋆)c(x,y)=c(x,y_\star)+c(x_\star,y)-c(x_\star,y_\star)c(x,y)=c(x,y⋆​)+c(x⋆​,y)−c(x⋆​,y⋆​) on the support, so the cost of any coupling depends only on its marginals.

If there exists a map T:X→YT:\Xx\to\YyT:X→Y with T♯α=βT_\sharp\al=\beT♯​α=β, then the Monge map induces the graph coupling π=(Id,T)♯α∈Π(α,β)\pi=(\Id,T)_\sharp\al\in\Couplings(\al,\be)π=(Id,T)♯​α∈Π(α,β), characterized by

∫h(x,y) dπ(x,y)=∫h(x,T(x)) dα(x).\int h(x,y)\d\pi(x,y) = \int h(x,T(x))\d\al(x).∫h(x,y)dπ(x,y)=∫h(x,T(x))dα(x).
(19)

Graph couplings are precisely the Kantorovich representation of deterministic Monge maps.

Continuous Kantorovich Problem¶

The discrete Kantorovich problem becomes, for arbitrary measures,

Lc(α,β):=inf⁡π∈Π(α,β)∫X×Yc(x,y) dπ(x,y).\mathcal{L}_c(\al,\be) \eqdef \inf_{\pi\in\Couplings(\al,\be)} \int_{\Xx\times\Yy} c(x,y)\d\pi(x,y).Lc​(α,β):=π∈Π(α,β)inf​∫X×Y​c(x,y)dπ(x,y).
(20)

This is an infinite-dimensional linear program over a space of measures.

Remark: Probabilistic Interpretation

The same problem can be written as

Lc(α,β)=inf⁡X∼α,  Y∼βE c(X,Y).\mathcal{L}_c(\al,\be) = \inf_{X\sim\al,\;Y\sim\be} \mathbb{E}\,c(X,Y).Lc​(α,β)=X∼α,Y∼βinf​Ec(X,Y).
(21)

The minimization is over all possible dependences between the two random variables, not over the fixed marginal laws.

Proposition: Existence On Compact Spaces

Assume that X\XxX and Y\YyY are compact metric spaces and c∈C(X×Y)c\in\Cc(\Xx\times\Yy)c∈C(X×Y). Then the Kantorovich problem admits at least one minimizer.

Proof

The constraint set is nonempty because it contains α⊗β\al\otimes\beα⊗β. It is closed for weak convergence because the marginal constraints are preserved. Since X×Y\Xx\times\YyX×Y is compact, probability measures on it are compact for the weak topology, and Π(α,β)\Couplings(\al,\be)Π(α,β) is compact. Finally, π↦∫c dπ\pi\mapsto\int c\d\piπ↦∫cdπ is weakly continuous because ccc is continuous and bounded.

On non-compact domains, one needs coercivity and moment conditions. For the Wasserstein cost c(x,y)=d(x,y)pc(x,y)=d(x,y)^pc(x,y)=d(x,y)p on a Polish metric space, the natural domain is

Pp(X):={μ∈M+1(X):∫d(x,x0)p dμ(x)<+∞},\mathcal P_p(\Xx) \eqdef \left\{ \mu\in\Mm_+^1(\Xx): \int d(x,x_0)^p\d\mu(x)<+\infty \right\},Pp​(X):={μ∈M+1​(X):∫d(x,x0​)pdμ(x)<+∞},
(22)

for one, and hence every, reference point x0x_0x0​.

Interpolation Induced By A Plan¶

When X=Y⊂Rd\Xx=\Yy\subset\RR^dX=Y⊂Rd, a transport plan defines an interpolation between measures by moving every active pair (x,y)(x,y)(x,y) along the segment (1−t)x+ty(1-t)x+ty(1−t)x+ty. If Tt(x,y)=(1−t)x+tyT_t(x,y)=(1-t)x+tyTt​(x,y)=(1−t)x+ty, then αt=(Tt)♯π\al_t=(T_t)_\sharp\piαt​=(Tt​)♯​π. In the discrete case, each mass PijP_{ij}Pij​ moves from xix_ixi​ to yjy_jyj​ along its own segment. When the plan is not induced by a map, one source atom can split into several moving atoms.

show_book_figure("kantorovich-plan-interpolation")

McCann interpolation induced by a non-deterministic transport plan. In every panel, the red and blue endpoint measures are shown with low opacity, thin gray segments display the support Pij>tolP_{ij}>\mathrm{tol}Pij​>tol of the coupling, and the moving atoms are colored from red to blue along the interpolation.

The companion panel lets the same coupling be inspected along time ttt, with an entropy slider to contrast sparse and diffuse plans.

Interactive panel. Use the interpolation time and plan controls to see how a fixed coupling induces a cloud of displacement paths between endpoint measures.

Monge--Kantorovich Equivalence¶

The proof of Brenier’s theorem relies on Kantorovich relaxation and duality. Under Brenier’s hypotheses, the relaxation is tight: it has the same cost as the Monge problem and the optimal coupling is induced by a map.

Corollary: Monge--Kantorovich Equivalence Under Brenier

Assume that α\alα is absolutely continuous with respect to Lebesgue measure and that c(x,y)=∥x−y∥2c(x,y)=\|x-y\|^2c(x,y)=∥x−y∥2. If TTT is the Brenier map solving Monge’s problem, then π=(Id,T)♯α\pi=(\Id,T)_\sharp\alπ=(Id,T)♯​α is the unique optimal coupling solving Kantorovich’s problem. In particular, the Monge and Kantorovich costs are the same.

Proof

The proof of Brenier’s theorem shows that the support of any optimal Kantorovich plan lies in the subdifferential ∂ϕ\partial\phi∂ϕ of a convex function. Since α\alα has a density, ϕ\phiϕ is differentiable α\alα-almost everywhere, so ∂ϕ(x)={∇ϕ(x)}\partial\phi(x)=\{\nabla\phi(x)\}∂ϕ(x)={∇ϕ(x)} for α\alα-almost every xxx. Every optimal coupling is therefore concentrated on the graph of T=∇ϕT=\nabla\phiT=∇ϕ and equals (Id,T)♯α(\Id,T)_\sharp\al(Id,T)♯​α.

If α\alα does not have a density, non-smooth points of ϕ\phiϕ can be charged by α\alα and mass splitting can occur. For instance, moving δ0\delta_0δ0​ to (δ−1+δ1)/2(\delta_{-1}+\delta_1)/2(δ−1​+δ1​)/2 can be represented by a plan concentrated on the set-valued subdifferential of ϕ(x)=∣x∣\phi(x)=|x|ϕ(x)=∣x∣, but not by a deterministic map.

Cyclical Monotonicity¶

Cyclical monotonicity is the local geometric fingerprint of optimality for a cost ccc. It converts a global minimization problem into finite exchange inequalities and is the bridge from Kantorovich plans to convex potentials.

Definition: Support

For a Radon measure π\piπ on X×Y\Xx\times\YyX×Y,

supp⁡(π):={(x,y):π(U×V)>0 for every open U∋x, V∋y}.\supp(\pi) \eqdef \{(x,y):\pi(U\times V)>0 \text{ for every open }U\ni x,\ V\ni y\}.supp(π):={(x,y):π(U×V)>0 for every open U∋x, V∋y}.
(23)
Definition: ccc-Cyclical Monotonicity

A set Γ⊂X×Y\Gamma\subset\Xx\times\YyΓ⊂X×Y is ccc-cyclically monotone if, for every k≥2k\geq2k≥2, every finite family (xi,yi)i=1k⊂Γ(x_i,y_i)_{i=1}^k\subset\Gamma(xi​,yi​)i=1k​⊂Γ and every permutation σ\sigmaσ of {1,…,k}\{1,\ldots,k\}{1,…,k},

∑i=1kc(xi,yi)≤∑i=1kc(xi,yσ(i)).\sum_{i=1}^k c(x_i,y_i) \leq \sum_{i=1}^k c(x_i,y_{\sigma(i)}).i=1∑k​c(xi​,yi​)≤i=1∑k​c(xi​,yσ(i)​).
(24)

It is enough to check cyclic permutations:

∑i=1kc(xi,yi)≤∑i=1kc(xi,yi+1),yk+1=y1.\sum_{i=1}^k c(x_i,y_i) \leq \sum_{i=1}^k c(x_i,y_{i+1}), \qquad y_{k+1}=y_1.i=1∑k​c(xi​,yi​)≤i=1∑k​c(xi​,yi+1​),yk+1​=y1​.
(25)
Theorem: Optimal Plans Are ccc-Cyclically Monotone

Assume ccc is continuous. For any optimal plan π\piπ solving the Kantorovich problem, supp⁡(π)\supp(\pi)supp(π) is ccc-cyclically monotone.

Proof Sketch

Suppose a finite family in supp⁡(π)\supp(\pi)supp(π) violates the exchange inequality. By continuity, the same strict inequality holds in small neighborhoods Ui×ViU_i\times V_iUi​×Vi​ around the chosen pairs. Remove a small equal amount of mass from each rectangle, keep its first and second marginal pieces, and reinsert the mass after permuting the second marginal pieces. The new measure has the same marginals but strictly smaller cost, contradicting optimality.

If the optimal plan is induced by a map TTT, cyclical monotonicity reads

∑i=1kc(xi,T(xi))≤∑i=1kc(xi,T(xi+1)).\sum_{i=1}^k c(x_i,T(x_i)) \leq \sum_{i=1}^k c(x_i,T(x_{i+1})).i=1∑k​c(xi​,T(xi​))≤i=1∑k​c(xi​,T(xi+1​)).
(26)

For c(x,y)=12∥x−y∥2c(x,y)=\frac12\|x-y\|^2c(x,y)=21​∥x−y∥2, the two-point case gives

⟨T(x)−T(y),x−y⟩≥0,\langle T(x)-T(y),x-y\rangle\geq0,⟨T(x)−T(y),x−y⟩≥0,
(27)

so TTT is a monotone vector field. In one dimension, for c(x,y)=∣x−y∣pc(x,y)=|x-y|^pc(x,y)=∣x−y∣p, this reduces to the classical monotone rearrangement.

Metric Properties: Wasserstein Distances¶

OT costs become genuine distances when the ground cost comes from a metric. The proof relies on a gluing lemma.

Lemma: Discrete Gluing Lemma

Given a∈Δna\in\simplex_na∈Δn​, b∈Δpb\in\simplex_pb∈Δp​, c∈Δmc\in\simplex_mc∈Δm​, P∈U(a,b)P\in\CouplingsD(a,b)P∈U(a,b) and Q∈U(b,c)Q\in\CouplingsD(b,c)Q∈U(b,c), define

R=Pdiag⁡(1/b)Q,Rik=∑j:bj>0PijQjkbj.R=P\diag(1/b)Q, \qquad R_{ik}=\sum_{j:b_j>0}\frac{P_{ij}Q_{jk}}{b_j}.R=Pdiag(1/b)Q,Rik​=j:bj​>0∑​bj​Pij​Qjk​​.
(28)

Then R∈U(a,c)R\in\CouplingsD(a,c)R∈U(a,c). It is the first-third marginal of the tensor coupling

Sijk={PijQjk/bj,bj>0,0,bj=0.S_{ijk} = \begin{cases} P_{ij}Q_{jk}/b_j, & b_j>0,\\ 0, & b_j=0. \end{cases}Sijk​={Pij​Qjk​/bj​,0,​bj​>0,bj​=0.​
(29)
Proof

If bj>0b_j>0bj​>0, summing SijkS_{ijk}Sijk​ over kkk gives Pijbj/bj=PijP_{ij}b_j/b_j=P_{ij}Pij​bj​/bj​=Pij​; if bj=0b_j=0bj​=0, the corresponding column of PPP and row of QQQ are zero. The other prescribed marginal is checked in the same way. Summing over the intermediate index jjj gives RRR. Its row and column sums are aaa and ccc.

show_book_figure("kantorovich-discrete-gluing-lemma")

Discrete gluing lemma in matrix form. The first two panels are optimal one-dimensional couplings through an intermediate marginal. The third panel shows the induced marginal R=Pdiag⁡(1/b)QR=P\diag(1/b)QR=Pdiag(1/b)Q; it is feasible and is the coupling used in the triangle-inequality proof.

The interactive version changes the resolution of the intermediate marginal, which controls how mediated the glued source-target plan becomes.

Interactive panel. Use the mediation slider to inspect how two couplings through an intermediate marginal glue into a source-target plan.

Proposition: Discrete Wasserstein Distance

Let DDD be a distance matrix on {1,…,n}\{1,\ldots,n\}{1,…,n} and p≥1p\geq1p≥1. Then

Wp(a,b):=(min⁡P∈U(a,b)∑i,jDijpPij)1/pW_p(a,b)\eqdef \left( \min_{P\in\CouplingsD(a,b)} \sum_{i,j} D_{ij}^p P_{ij} \right)^{1/p}Wp​(a,b):=(P∈U(a,b)min​i,j∑​Dijp​Pij​)1/p
(30)

defines a distance on Δn\simplex_nΔn​.

Proof

Symmetry follows by transposing couplings. Positivity follows because a zero cost plan must be supported on the diagonal. For the triangle inequality, take optimal couplings PPP from aaa to bbb and QQQ from bbb to ccc, glue them into SSS, and use the feasible marginal RRR from aaa to ccc. Then Minkowski’s inequality and the ground triangle inequality give

Wp(a,c)≤(∑i,j,k(Dij+Djk)pSijk)1/p≤Wp(a,b)+Wp(b,c).W_p(a,c) \leq \left(\sum_{i,j,k}(D_{ij}+D_{jk})^pS_{ijk}\right)^{1/p} \leq W_p(a,b)+W_p(b,c).Wp​(a,c)≤⎝⎛​i,j,k∑​(Dij​+Djk​)pSijk​⎠⎞​1/p≤Wp​(a,b)+Wp​(b,c).
(31)
Lemma: Gluing Lemma

Let (α,β,γ)(\al,\be,\ga)(α,β,γ) be probability measures on Polish spaces. Given π∈Π(α,β)\pi\in\Couplings(\al,\be)π∈Π(α,β) and ξ∈Π(β,γ)\xi\in\Couplings(\be,\ga)ξ∈Π(β,γ), there exists a measure σ\sigmaσ on X×Y×Z\Xx\times\Yy\times\ZzX×Y×Z such that

(PX,Y)♯σ=π,(PY,Z)♯σ=ξ.(P_{\Xx,\Yy})_\sharp\sigma=\pi, \qquad (P_{\Yy,\Zz})_\sharp\sigma=\xi.(PX,Y​)♯​σ=π,(PY,Z​)♯​σ=ξ.
(32)
Proof Sketch

Disintegrate π\piπ and ξ\xiξ against their common marginal β\beβ, obtaining conditional laws πy\pi_yπy​ on X\XxX and ξy\xi_yξy​ on Z\ZzZ. Define σ\sigmaσ by the conditional-product formula

∫g(x,y,z) dσ=∫Y(∫X∫Zg(x,y,z) dπy(x) dξy(z)) dβ(y).\int g(x,y,z)\d\sigma = \int_\Yy \left(\int_\Xx\int_\Zz g(x,y,z)\d\pi_y(x)\d\xi_y(z)\right) \d\be(y).∫g(x,y,z)dσ=∫Y​(∫X​∫Z​g(x,y,z)dπy​(x)dξy​(z))dβ(y).
(33)

This is the measure-theoretic version of the discrete formula above.

Proposition: Wasserstein Distance On Measures

Assume X=Y\X=\YX=Y and ddd is a distance on X\XX. For p≥1p\geq1p≥1,

Wp(α,β):=(inf⁡π∈Π(α,β)∫d(x,y)p dπ(x,y))1/p\Wass_p(\al,\be) \eqdef \left( \inf_{\pi\in\Couplings(\al,\be)} \int d(x,y)^p\d\pi(x,y) \right)^{1/p}Wp​(α,β):=(π∈Π(α,β)inf​∫d(x,y)pdπ(x,y))1/p
(34)

defines the ppp-Wasserstein distance.

Proof

Symmetry is obtained by swapping the coordinates of a coupling. If the value is zero, an optimal coupling is supported on the diagonal and therefore the two marginals coincide. For the triangle inequality, glue optimal couplings π∈Π(α,β)\pi\in\Couplings(\al,\be)π∈Π(α,β) and ξ∈Π(β,γ)\xi\in\Couplings(\be,\ga)ξ∈Π(β,γ) into σ\sigmaσ, project it to a coupling ρ\rhoρ between α\alα and γ\gaγ, and apply the ground triangle inequality plus Minkowski:

Wp(α,γ)≤(∫(d(x,y)+d(y,z))p dσ(x,y,z))1/p≤Wp(α,β)+Wp(β,γ).\Wass_p(\al,\ga) \leq \left(\int (d(x,y)+d(y,z))^p\d\sigma(x,y,z)\right)^{1/p} \leq \Wass_p(\al,\be)+\Wass_p(\be,\ga).Wp​(α,γ)≤(∫(d(x,y)+d(y,z))pdσ(x,y,z))1/p≤Wp​(α,β)+Wp​(β,γ).
(35)

Kantorovich’s distance should be contrasted with the directed Monge distance. The Kantorovich feasible set is never empty, although the cost may be infinite without moment assumptions. When an optimal Monge map exists, the graph coupling gives the same value.

Metric Properties: Topology And Applications¶

Wasserstein distances metrize weak convergence under moment control, sit between weak and strong topologies, and provide quantitative estimates in probability and robust optimization.

Proposition: Equivalence Of Wasserstein Distances On Compact Spaces

On a compact metric space, for p≤qp\leq qp≤q,

Wp(α,β)≤Wq(α,β)≤diam⁡(X)(q−p)/qWp(α,β)p/q.\Wass_p(\al,\be) \leq \Wass_q(\al,\be) \leq \diam(\Xx)^{(q-p)/q}\Wass_p(\al,\be)^{p/q}.Wp​(α,β)≤Wq​(α,β)≤diam(X)(q−p)/qWp​(α,β)p/q.
(36)
Proof

The left inequality is Jensen’s inequality applied to r↦rq/pr\mapsto r^{q/p}r↦rq/p. The right inequality follows from d(x,y)q≤diam⁡(X)q−pd(x,y)pd(x,y)^q\leq\diam(\Xx)^{q-p}d(x,y)^pd(x,y)q≤diam(X)q−pd(x,y)p.

Definition: Weak∗^*∗ Topology

A sequence (αk)k(\al_k)_k(αk​)k​ converges weakly∗^*∗ to α\alα in M+1(X)\Mm_+^1(\Xx)M+1​(X) if, for every bounded continuous function fff,

∫f dαk⟶∫f dα.\int f\d\al_k\longrightarrow\int f\d\al.∫fdαk​⟶∫fdα.
(37)
Remark: A Riemann-Sum Weak Limit

On X=R\Xx=\RRX=R,

1n∑k=1nδk/n⇀U[0,1].\frac1n\sum_{k=1}^n\delta_{k/n} \rightharpoonup \mathcal U_{[0,1]}.n1​k=1∑n​δk/n​⇀U[0,1]​.
(38)

This is exactly convergence of Riemann sums. The convergence is weak but not strong: the empirical measure and the uniform density are mutually singular, so their total variation distance is 2.

Remark: Weak Convergence For Discrete Measures

For a single Dirac, δx(n)⇀δx\delta_{x^{(n)}}\rightharpoonup\delta_xδx(n)​⇀δx​ is equivalent to x(n)→xx^{(n)}\to xx(n)→x. With a fixed number of atoms, convergence of locations and weights gives weak convergence after relabeling and merging identical limits. Without a uniform bound on the number of atoms, weak limits of discrete measures can be continuous; empirical measures are the standard example.

Remark: Modes Of Convergence For Random Variables

If XnX_nXn​ and XXX live on a common probability space, almost-sure convergence implies convergence in probability, and convergence in probability implies convergence in law. Convergence in law is exactly weak convergence of the laws (Xn)♯P⇀X♯P(X_n)_\sharp\PP\rightharpoonup X_\sharp\PP(Xn​)♯​P⇀X♯​P, and does not require all variables to live on the same probability space.

Remark: Central Limit Theorem

If (X1,…,Xn)(X_1,\ldots,X_n)(X1​,…,Xn​) are i.i.d. random vectors with finite second moments, EXi=0\mathbb{E}X_i=0EXi​=0 and E(XiXi⊤)=Id\mathbb{E}(X_iX_i^\top)=\IdE(Xi​Xi⊤​)=Id, then

Zn:=1n∑i=1nXiZ_n\eqdef\frac1{\sqrt n}\sum_{i=1}^n X_iZn​:=n​1​i=1∑n​Xi​
(39)

converges in law toward N(0,Id)\Gaussian(0,\Id)N(0,Id).

Proposition: Total Variation As Wasserstein For The Discrete Metric

Let ddd be the 0/10/10/1 metric, with d(x,x)=0d(x,x)=0d(x,x)=0 and d(x,y)=1d(x,y)=1d(x,y)=1 for x≠yx\neq yx=y. Then

Wp(α,β)p=12∥α−β∥TV.\Wass_p(\al,\be)^p = \frac12\|\al-\be\|_{\TV}.Wp​(α,β)p=21​∥α−β∥TV​.
(40)
Proof Sketch

For discrete measures on a common support, set ci=min⁡(ai,bi)c_i=\min(a_i,b_i)ci​=min(ai​,bi​). Any coupling has diagonal mass at most ∑ici\sum_i c_i∑i​ci​, so its off-diagonal cost is at least 1−∑ici1-\sum_i c_i1−∑i​ci​. This bound is achieved by putting cic_ici​ on the diagonal and coupling the leftover positive and negative parts by a product plan. Since

1−∑ici=12∑i∣ai−bi∣,1-\sum_i c_i = \frac12\sum_i |a_i-b_i|,1−i∑​ci​=21​i∑​∣ai​−bi​∣,
(41)

the formula follows.

For Dirac masses,

∥δxn−δx∥TV=2,Wp(δxn,δx)=d(xn,x).\|\delta_{x_n}-\delta_x\|_{\TV}=2, \qquad \Wass_p(\delta_{x_n},\delta_x)=d(x_n,x).∥δxn​​−δx​∥TV​=2,Wp​(δxn​​,δx​)=d(xn​,x).
(42)

Thus the strong topology never sees Diracs converge unless they are eventually equal, while the Wasserstein topology captures their spatial convergence.

Proposition: Wasserstein Metrizes Weak Convergence On Compact Spaces

If X\XxX is compact, then αk⇀α\al_k\rightharpoonup\alαk​⇀α if and only if Wp(αk,α)→0\Wass_p(\al_k,\al)\to0Wp​(αk​,α)→0.

Proof Sketch

For p=1p=1p=1, this is the Kantorovich--Rubinstein metrization theorem: by duality, W1\Wass_1W1​ is the supremum over 1-Lipschitz test functions, and on a compact metric space this class is compact modulo constants by Arzela--Ascoli. The comparison between Wp\Wass_pWp​ distances on compact spaces then gives the result for all p≥1p\geq1p≥1.

On non-compact spaces, one must also impose convergence of ppp-th moments: Wp(αk,α)→0\Wass_p(\alpha_k,\alpha)\to0Wp​(αk​,α)→0 if and only if αk⇀α\alpha_k\rightharpoonup\alphaαk​⇀α and

∫d(x,x0)p dαk(x)→∫d(x,x0)p dα(x).\int d(x,x_0)^p\d\alpha_k(x) \to \int d(x,x_0)^p\d\alpha(x).∫d(x,x0​)pdαk​(x)→∫d(x,x0​)pdα(x).
(43)

On a discrete space, weak and strong topologies coincide, and

dmin⁡2∥α−β∥TV≤W1(α,β)≤dmax⁡2∥α−β∥TV.\frac{d_{\min}}{2}\|\al-\be\|_{\TV} \leq \Wass_1(\al,\be) \leq \frac{d_{\max}}{2}\|\al-\be\|_{\TV}.2dmin​​∥α−β∥TV​≤W1​(α,β)≤2dmax​​∥α−β∥TV​.
(44)

Wasserstein Over Wasserstein¶

The construction can be iterated. Once (X,d)(\X,d)(X,d) is a metric space, the set of probability measures on X\XX becomes a metric space through Wp\Wass_pWp​. It can therefore serve as a new ground space. This is useful whenever the objects to compare are themselves random probability measures, or mixtures whose components are meaningful objects rather than only a collapsed density.

Proposition: Wasserstein Spaces As Ground Spaces

If (X,d)(\X,d)(X,d) is a Polish metric space, then Pp(X)\Pp_p(\X)Pp​(X) endowed with Wp\Wass_pWp​ is Polish. If X\XX is compact, then P(X)\Pp(\X)P(X) is compact for the Wasserstein topology, and the construction can be iterated to form P(P(X))\Pp(\Pp(\X))P(P(X)), P(P(P(X)))\Pp(\Pp(\Pp(\X)))P(P(P(X))), and so on.

Proof Sketch

Completeness follows by representing a Wp\Wass_pWp​-Cauchy sequence through almost optimally glued couplings, which gives a Cauchy random sequence whose law is the desired limit. Separability follows by approximating measures with finitely supported measures on a countable dense subset and rational weights. If X\XX is compact, Prokhorov compactness and Wasserstein metrization of weak convergence give compactness.

Elements of P2(P2(X))\Pp_2(\Pp_2(\X))P2​(P2​(X)) are probability laws over probability measures, or random probability measures. A parametric example is

A=(ζ↦αζ)♯γ.\mathfrak A=(\zeta\mapsto\alpha_\zeta)_\sharp\gamma.A=(ζ↦αζ​)♯​γ.
(45)

The collapsed mixture associated with A\mathfrak AA is

∫Xf(x) dαˉA(x)=∫P2(X)(∫Xf(x) dα(x)) dA(α).\int_\X f(x)\d\bar\alpha_{\mathfrak A}(x) = \int_{\Pp_2(\X)} \left(\int_\X f(x)\d\alpha(x)\right) \d\mathfrak A(\alpha).∫X​f(x)dαˉA​(x)=∫P2​(X)​(∫X​f(x)dα(x))dA(α).
(46)

The Wasserstein distance on the Wasserstein space is

W22(A,B):=inf⁡Π∈Π(A,B)∫P2(X)×P2(X)W22(α,β) dΠ(α,β).\mathbb W_2^2(\mathfrak A,\mathfrak B) \eqdef \inf_{\Pi\in\Couplings(\mathfrak A,\mathfrak B)} \int_{\Pp_2(\X)\times\Pp_2(\X)} \Wass_2^2(\alpha,\beta)\d\Pi(\alpha,\beta).W22​(A,B):=Π∈Π(A,B)inf​∫P2​(X)×P2​(X)​W22​(α,β)dΠ(α,β).
(47)

For Gaussian mixtures, this separates two geometries. A mixture can be viewed as a collapsed density on X\XX, or as a component law over Gaussian atoms in the Bures--Wasserstein space. Interpolating at the component level generally differs from the true W2\Wass_2W2​ interpolation between the collapsed mixture densities.

show_book_figure("kantorovich-wow-mixtures")

Two interpolations between the same three-component one-dimensional Gaussian mixtures. On the left, components are matched using the Bures--Wasserstein distance between Gaussians. On the right, the mixtures are collapsed into ordinary one-dimensional densities and interpolated by the true quantile formula for W2\Wass_2W2​.

The interactive comparison keeps both geometries side by side: component-level transport moves Gaussian atoms, while collapsed transport rearranges the full density.

Interactive panel. Use the mixture and blur controls to compare transport between ordinary measures with transport between distributions of measures.

Proposition: Collapsing Is Non-Expansive

Let A,B∈P2(P2(X))\mathfrak A,\mathfrak B\in\Pp_2(\Pp_2(\X))A,B∈P2​(P2​(X)), and let αˉA\bar\alpha_{\mathfrak A}αˉA​ and βˉB\bar\beta_{\mathfrak B}βˉ​B​ be the collapsed mixtures. Then

W2(αˉA,βˉB)≤W2(A,B).\Wass_2(\bar\alpha_{\mathfrak A},\bar\beta_{\mathfrak B}) \leq \mathbb W_2(\mathfrak A,\mathfrak B).W2​(αˉA​,βˉ​B​)≤W2​(A,B).
(48)
Proof

Fix a coupling Π\PiΠ between A\mathfrak AA and B\mathfrak BB. For each (α,β)(\alpha,\beta)(α,β) choose an almost optimal coupling πα,β∈Π(α,β)\pi_{\alpha,\beta}\in\Couplings(\alpha,\beta)πα,β​∈Π(α,β). Integrating this Markov kernel against Π\PiΠ gives a coupling between the collapsed mixtures whose cost is bounded by the Π\PiΠ-average of W22(α,β)\Wass_2^2(\alpha,\beta)W22​(α,β). Taking infima proves the claim.

This viewpoint also clarifies lower bounds for Gromov--Wasserstein distances: a metric-measure space can be mapped to a law of local distance profiles, and these laws can be compared by Wasserstein-over-Wasserstein.

Distributional Robustness And Wasserstein Infinity¶

Wasserstein distances define ambiguity sets around empirical laws. Given samples ziz_izi​ and α^n=1n∑iδzi\hat\alpha_n=\frac1n\sum_i\delta_{z_i}α^n​=n1​∑i​δzi​​, a distributionally robust optimization problem replaces empirical risk by

sup⁡β:Wp(β,α^n)≤ρ∫ℓθ(z) dβ(z).\sup_{\beta:\Wass_p(\beta,\hat\alpha_n)\leq\rho} \int \ell_\theta(z)\d\beta(z).β:Wp​(β,α^n​)≤ρsup​∫ℓθ​(z)dβ(z).
(49)

Under standard upper-semicontinuity and growth assumptions on the loss, one has the dual reformulation

sup⁡β:Wp(β,α^n)p≤ρp∫ℓθ dβ=inf⁡λ≥0λρp+1n∑i=1nsup⁡z{ℓθ(z)−λd(z,zi)p}.\sup_{\beta:\Wass_p(\beta,\hat\alpha_n)^p\leq\rho^p} \int \ell_\theta\d\beta = \inf_{\lambda\geq0} \lambda\rho^p + \frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n \sup_z\{\ell_\theta(z)-\lambda d(z,z_i)^p\}.β:Wp​(β,α^n​)p≤ρpsup​∫ℓθ​dβ=λ≥0inf​λρp+n1​i=1∑n​zsup​{ℓθ​(z)−λd(z,zi​)p}.
(50)

The robust risk is therefore an empirical risk in which each sample is replaced by its worst penalized perturbation. For p=1p=1p=1 and an LθL_\thetaLθ​-Lipschitz loss,

sup⁡β:W1(β,α^n)≤ρ∫ℓθ dβ≤1n∑iℓθ(zi)+ρLθ.\sup_{\beta:\Wass_1(\beta,\hat\alpha_n)\leq\rho} \int \ell_\theta\d\beta \leq \frac1n\sum_i\ell_\theta(z_i)+\rho L_\theta.β:W1​(β,α^n​)≤ρsup​∫ℓθ​dβ≤n1​i∑​ℓθ​(zi​)+ρLθ​.
(51)
Proposition: Convexity Of Transport Costs

For any nonnegative lower-semicontinuous cost ccc, the value

(α,β)↦Lc(α,β)(\alpha,\beta)\mapsto\mathcal{L}_c(\alpha,\beta)(α,β)↦Lc​(α,β)
(52)

is jointly convex. In particular, (α,β)↦Wp(α,β)p(\alpha,\beta)\mapsto\Wass_p(\alpha,\beta)^p(α,β)↦Wp​(α,β)p is jointly convex. The distance W1\Wass_1W1​ is jointly convex, but Wp\Wass_pWp​ itself need not be convex for p>1p>1p>1.

Proof

Let π0\pi_0π0​ and π1\pi_1π1​ be nearly optimal couplings for (α0,β0)(\alpha_0,\beta_0)(α0​,β0​) and (α1,β1)(\alpha_1,\beta_1)(α1​,β1​). Then (1−t)π0+tπ1(1-t)\pi_0+t\pi_1(1−t)π0​+tπ1​ is a coupling between the convex combinations of the marginals, and its cost is the corresponding convex combination. Taking infima proves joint convexity. For p>1p>1p>1, the root can destroy convexity; on the line, Wp((1−t)δ0+tδ1,δ0)=t1/p\Wass_p((1-t)\delta_0+t\delta_1,\delta_0)=t^{1/p}Wp​((1−t)δ0​+tδ1​,δ0​)=t1/p is concave.

The limiting distance

W∞(α,β):=inf⁡π∈Π(α,β)ess sup⁡(x,y)∼πd(x,y)\Wass_\infty(\alpha,\beta) \eqdef \inf_{\pi\in\Couplings(\alpha,\beta)} \esssup_{(x,y)\sim\pi} d(x,y)W∞​(α,β):=π∈Π(α,β)inf​(x,y)∼πesssup​d(x,y)
(53)

minimizes the worst displacement rather than an average displacement.

Proposition: W∞\Wass_\inftyW∞​ Robust Envelope Around An Empirical Law

Let (Z,d)(\Zz,d)(Z,d) be a Polish metric space. Let α^=∑i=1naiδzi\hat\alpha=\sum_{i=1}^n a_i\delta_{z_i}α^=∑i=1n​ai​δzi​​ with ai>0a_i>0ai​>0 and ∑iai=1\sum_i a_i=1∑i​ai​=1, and assume the closed balls B‾(zi,ρ)\overline B(z_i,\rho)B(zi​,ρ) are compact. For any real-valued upper-semicontinuous loss ℓ\ellℓ,

sup⁡β:W∞(β,α^)≤ρ∫ℓ(z) dβ(z)=∑i=1naisup⁡z∈B‾(zi,ρ)ℓ(z).\sup_{\beta:\Wass_\infty(\beta,\hat\alpha)\leq\rho} \int \ell(z)\d\beta(z) = \sum_{i=1}^n a_i \sup_{z\in\overline B(z_i,\rho)}\ell(z).β:W∞​(β,α^)≤ρsup​∫ℓ(z)dβ(z)=i=1∑n​ai​z∈B(zi​,ρ)sup​ℓ(z).
(54)
Proof

Any feasible coupling between α^\hat\alphaα^ and β\betaβ disintegrates as ∑iaiδzi⊗νi\sum_i a_i\delta_{z_i}\otimes\nu_i∑i​ai​δzi​​⊗νi​, with each νi\nu_iνi​ supported in B‾(zi,ρ)\overline B(z_i,\rho)B(zi​,ρ). Hence the robust expectation is bounded above by the displayed sum. The reverse inequality follows by choosing, for each iii, a maximizer zi⋆∈B‾(zi,ρ)z_i^\star\in\overline B(z_i,\rho)zi⋆​∈B(zi​,ρ) and taking β=∑iaiδzi⋆\beta=\sum_i a_i\delta_{z_i^\star}β=∑i​ai​δzi⋆​​.

Theoretical Application: Quantitative Central Limit Theorems¶

Weak topology says whether laws converge; Wasserstein distances also quantify how fast. The central limit theorem becomes a rate estimate in W1\Wass_1W1​, which controls the error of all 1-Lipschitz observables of the normalized sum.

show_book_figure("matching-quantitative-clt")

Central-limit theorem for normalized Bernoulli sums. Starting from α0=12(δ−1+δ1)\alpha_0=\frac12(\delta_{-1}+\delta_1)α0​=21​(δ−1​+δ1​), the law of Zn=n−1/2∑iXiZ_n=n^{-1/2}\sum_i X_iZn​=n−1/2∑i​Xi​ remains discrete, but its rescaled atom heights approach the standard Gaussian density shown in gray.

The interactive demo varies the number of summands and the Bernoulli skew, making the Wasserstein view of weak convergence visible even while the law remains discrete.

Interactive panel. Use the dimension and sample-size controls to watch the Wasserstein CLT scaling predicted by Lipschitz test functions.

Proposition: Berry--Esseen Bound In W1\Wass_1W1​

Let (Xi)i=1n(X_i)_{i=1}^n(Xi​)i=1n​ be i.i.d. real random variables with EXi=0\mathbb{E}X_i=0EXi​=0, EXi2=1\mathbb{E}X_i^2=1EXi2​=1 and E∣Xi∣3<+∞\mathbb{E}|X_i|^3<+\inftyE∣Xi​∣3<+∞. If αn\alpha_nαn​ is the law of n−1/2∑iXin^{-1/2}\sum_i X_in−1/2∑i​Xi​ and γ\gammaγ is the standard Gaussian law, then

W1(αn,γ)≤C E∣X1∣3n,\Wass_1(\alpha_n,\gamma) \leq \frac{C\,\mathbb{E}|X_1|^3}{\sqrt n},W1​(αn​,γ)≤n​CE∣X1​∣3​,
(55)

where CCC is a universal constant.

Proof Sketch

By Kantorovich--Rubinstein duality,

W1(αn,γ)=sup⁡Lip⁡(h)≤1∣Eh(Sn)−Eh(G)∣.\Wass_1(\alpha_n,\gamma) = \sup_{\Lip(h)\leq1} \left|\mathbb{E}h(S_n)-\mathbb{E}h(G)\right|.W1​(αn​,γ)=Lip(h)≤1sup​∣Eh(Sn​)−Eh(G)∣.
(56)

For each such hhh, solve Stein’s equation fh′(x)−xfh(x)=h(x)−Eh(G)f_h'(x)-xf_h(x)=h(x)-\mathbb{E}h(G)fh′​(x)−xfh​(x)=h(x)−Eh(G). The solution has uniform derivative bounds depending only on the Lipschitz constant of hhh. Expanding by replacing summands one at a time, the first- and second-order terms cancel because EXi=0\mathbb{E}X_i=0EXi​=0 and EXi2=1\mathbb{E}X_i^2=1EXi2​=1. The Taylor remainder is bounded by C∑iE∣Xi/n∣3C\sum_i\mathbb{E}|X_i/\sqrt n|^3C∑i​E∣Xi​/n​∣3, giving the n−1/2n^{-1/2}n−1/2 rate Berry, 1941Esseen, 1942Chen et al., 2011Bobkov, 2018Rio, 2011.

References¶
  1. Kantorovich, L. (1942). On the transfer of masses (in Russian). Doklady Akademii Nauk, 37(2), 227–229.
  2. Villani, C. (2003). Topics in Optimal Transportation (Vol. 58). American Mathematical Society.
  3. Villani, C. (2009). Optimal Transport: Old and New (Vol. 338). Springer.
  4. Rachev, S. T., & Rüschendorf, L. (1998). Mass Transportation Problems: Volume II: Applications. Springer.
  5. Dantzig, G. B. (1951). Application of the simplex method to a transportation problem. Activity Analysis of Production and Allocation, 13, 359–373.
  6. Bertsekas, D. P., & Eckstein, J. (1988). Dual coordinate step methods for linear network flow problems. Mathematical Programming, 42(1), 203–243.
  7. Orlin, J. B. (1997). A polynomial time primal network simplex algorithm for minimum cost flows. Mathematical Programming, 78(2), 109–129.
  8. Berry, A. C. (1941). The Accuracy of the Gaussian Approximation to the Sum of Independent Variates. Transactions of the American Mathematical Society, 49(1), 122–136.
  9. Esseen, C.-G. (1942). On the Liapunoff Limit of Error in the Theory of Probability. Arkiv for Matematik, Astronomi Och Fysik, 28A(9), 1–19.
  10. Chen, L. H. Y., Goldstein, L., & Shao, Q.-M. (2011). Normal Approximation by Stein’s Method. Springer.
  11. Bobkov, S. G. (2018). Berry–Esseen bounds and Edgeworth expansions in the central limit theorem for transport distances. Probability Theory and Related Fields, 170, 229–262.
  12. Rio, E. (2011). Asymptotic constants for minimal distance in the central limit theorem. Electronic Communications in Probability, 16, 96–103.
